also kind of apply to upper body workouts but more so lower body workouts so I say it in every single like workout split video but the way that I structure my leg workouts is I start with compounds go into unilateral movements and then go into isolation so a compound movement is a movement that targets more than one muscle at a time and it is usually the movements that require the most energy and usually the movements that you would want to progressively overload in more than a unilateral movement for example you'd want to like increase the weight on them each week increase the reps and that is your RDLs your hip thrusts your squats for example those are just three examples of a compound movement and you may find that when you do those movements say you do a hip thrust it's not just your bum that's hurting it's also your hamstrings it can also be some other muscles because it works more than one muscle at a time so I always always recommend doing these doing these movements at the start of your workout, just because obviously, as I said, they require the most energy and usually it's the workouts that you are wanting to progress in each week and you're not gonna be able to see the most progression and benefit the most from the exercises if you are putting them in at the end when you're depleted and tired. So yeah, I always to start with compounds and then go into unilateral movements which are the single leg or single arm movements so today i did a single leg dumbbell rdl and then i did cable step ups but for me cable step ups are also sort of an isolation movement as well for my glutes i would class them more as an isolation movement and unilateral movements are just super important to kind of try and prevent imbalances in your body as much as possible and it is just also really good to just put a lot of focus on one side of your body at one time which is great for obviously growth and it just gives more attention to the specific muscle rather than you know just doing compound movements where you're working a lot of muscles at once and then at the end i like to do my isolation movements so these are movements that focus more on one muscle at a time no exercise is ever going to literally just focus on one muscle but the isolation movements focus on one muscle more so than a compound for example they're just much more isolating on a specific muscle that you are wanting to contract for example a hamstring curl it is predominantly hamstring focused you'll be able to feel it if you've ever done the movement same as a leg extension when you're doing quads it's just very heavily focused on that one muscle and it's also just a really good way to burn out that muscle towards the end of your session it's the kind of movements that i would like to do more reps in and maybe go a little bit lighter on them like I'm not trying to max out on the hamstring curl or the leg extension they're the kind of things that I would maybe do drop sets with I'd do 12 to 15 reps with and just really burn out that muscle at the end just put a lot of strain on that one muscle but yeah that is how I going to do seated hamstring curl for three sets of 10 to 12 reps. all right guys my session is done please ignore the state of my hair it's just vile right now um but that glue session was actually pretty good like i actually enjoyed it i took my time with it because i've just found a lot recently that i've just been going oh i'll just rush this a little bit just so i can get back quicker i'll rush this i'll do one less set maybe and today i didn't do that and i was in there for quite a while not that you need to be in there for like a long time but i'm just glad that like i wasn't rushing things and i just enjoyed the session also pushed some pretty big weight as well um which i wasn't expecting so yeah i'm happy about that but yeah I just wanted to talk to you guys about the structure of my leg workout specifically this does also kind of apply to upper body workouts but more so lower body workouts so I say it in every single like workout split video but the way that I structure my leg workouts is I start with compounds go into unilateral movements and then go into isolation so a compound movement is a movement that targets more than one muscle at a time and it is usually the movements that require the most energy and usually the movements that you would want to progressively overload in more than a unilateral movement for example you'd want to like increase the weight on them each week increase the reps and that is your RDLs your hip thrusts your squats for example those are just three examples of a compound movement and you may find that when you do those movements say you do a hip thrust it's not just your bum that's hurting it's also your hamstrings it can also be some other muscles because it works more than one muscle at a time so I always always recommend doing these doing these movements at the start of your workout, just because obviously, as I said, they require the most energy and usually it's the workouts that you are wanting to progress in each week and you're not gonna be able to see the most progression and benefit the most from the exercises if you are putting them in at the end when you're depleted and tired. So yeah, I always to start with compounds and then go into unilateral movements which are the single leg or single arm movements so today i did a single leg dumbbell rdl and then i did cable step ups but for me cable step ups are also sort of an isolation movement as well for my glutes i would class them more as an isolation movement and unilateral movements are just super important to kind of try and prevent imbalances in your body as much as possible and it is just also really good to just put a lot of focus on one side of your body at one time which is great for obviously growth and it just gives more attention to the specific muscle rather than you know just doing compound movements where you're working a lot of muscles at once and then at the end i like to do my isolation movements so these are movements that focus more on one muscle at a time no exercise is ever going to literally just focus on one muscle but the isolation movements focus on one muscle more so than a compound for example they're just much more isolating on a specific muscle that you are wanting to contract for example a hamstring curl it is predominantly hamstring focused you'll be able to feel it if you've ever done the movement same as a leg extension when you're doing quads it's just very heavily focused on that one muscle and it's also just a really good way to burn out that muscle towards the end of your session it's the kind of movements that i would like to do more reps in and maybe go a little bit lighter on them like I'm not trying to max out on the hamstring curl or the leg extension they're the kind of things that I would maybe do drop sets with I'd do 12 to 15 reps with and just really burn out that muscle at the end just put a lot of strain on that one muscle but yeah that is how I advise to structure your leg workouts always I do not really recommend doing compounds at the end you know sometimes you might see people barbell squatting at the end and you know that's fine if you want to do that i just wouldn't really recommend it um but if your focus is elsewhere rather than progressing in those movements then you do you but if someone was to ask me how to structure a workout
